title: Storing Rotating Database Credentials
description:
## Managing Credentials That Must Rotate

A team runs an application that connects to an Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL database. Security policy requires that the database credentials be **stored securely**, retrieved by the application at runtime via IAM-controlled access, and **automatically rotated on a scheduled basis (every 30 days)** without code changes or downtime. The team wants the **least operational effort** to meet the rotation requirement. Which approach is BEST?

options.text: Store the credentials in AWS Secrets Manager and enable managed automatic rotation with the built-in RDS rotation Lambda on a 30-day schedule
Store the credentials as a plaintext String parameter in SSM Parameter Store and update them manually each month
Store the credentials in an SSM Parameter Store SecureString and rely on its built-in automatic rotation
Hardcode the credentials in the application configuration and redeploy when they need to change

explanation:
**AWS Secrets Manager** is purpose-built for secrets and is the only option here with **native automatic rotation**. For RDS/Aurora it provides a managed rotation Lambda and integration so you configure a rotation schedule (e.g., 30 days) and Secrets Manager rotates the credential in both the secret and the database — no app code change, and the application always fetches the current value via the API (IAM-controlled, KMS-encrypted at rest). This is the least-effort way to satisfy scheduled rotation.

(c) **SSM Parameter Store SecureString** encrypts the value with KMS and is cheaper, but it has **no built-in rotation** — you would have to build and schedule your own rotation logic, which is more operational effort, so the claim of 'built-in automatic rotation' is false. (b) A **plaintext** Parameter Store String stores the secret unencrypted and requires manual monthly updates — insecure and high-effort. (d) **Hardcoding** credentials is the worst option: it puts secrets in source/config, requires a redeploy to rotate, and violates the secure-storage requirement.

Rule of thumb: choose **Secrets Manager** when you need built-in rotation or secret-specific features; choose **Parameter Store SecureString** for cheaper config/secret storage when rotation is not required.

hints.title: What's the differentiator?
hints.body: Both Secrets Manager and Parameter Store SecureString encrypt with KMS. The deciding feature here is native, scheduled rotation.

hints.title: RDS integration
hints.body: Secrets Manager ships managed rotation functions for RDS/Aurora credentials out of the box.
